questions.question: What is land revenue?
questions.answer: Land revenue is the tax that farmers had to pay to the government for using agricultural land.

questions.question: Why was land revenue important for the British government?
questions.answer: Land revenue was the main source of income for the British administration in India.

questions.question: What was the Permanent Settlement?
questions.answer: The Permanent Settlement was a land revenue system introduced by Lord Cornwallis in 1793 in Bengal.

questions.question: Who were zamindars?
questions.answer: Zamindars were landowners who collected land revenue from peasants and paid it to the British government.

questions.question: What responsibilities did zamindars have under the Permanent Settlement?
questions.answer: Zamindars were responsible for collecting revenue from peasants and paying a fixed amount to the British government.

questions.question: Who were peasants?
questions.answer: Peasants were farmers who cultivated land and paid taxes to the zamindars or government.

questions.question: Why did many peasants fall into debt?
questions.answer: Peasants often borrowed money from moneylenders to pay high land taxes.

questions.question: What is indigo?
questions.answer: Indigo is a plant used to produce blue dye for colouring cloth.

questions.question: Why was indigo cultivation important for the British?
questions.answer: Indigo dye was in great demand in European markets for colouring textiles.

questions.question: Who were indigo planters?
questions.answer: Indigo planters were European landowners who forced Indian peasants to grow indigo.

questions.question: What was the advance system in indigo cultivation?
questions.answer: The advance system involved giving loans to peasants and forcing them to grow indigo crops.

questions.question: Why did peasants dislike growing indigo?
questions.answer: Indigo cultivation reduced food crop production and gave farmers very little profit.

questions.question: What was the Indigo Revolt?
questions.answer: The Indigo Revolt was a protest by peasants in Bengal against indigo planters in 1859–1860.

questions.question: Why did peasants start the Indigo Revolt?
questions.answer: Peasants revolted because they were forced to grow indigo and received very little profit.

questions.question: How did peasants protest during the Indigo Revolt?
questions.answer: Peasants refused to grow indigo and organised protests against the planters.

questions.question: What action did the British government take after the Indigo Revolt?
questions.answer: The British government appointed a commission to investigate the problems faced by indigo farmers.

questions.question: Explain the role of moneylenders in rural society.
questions.answer: Moneylenders provided loans to peasants, but high interest rates often trapped farmers in debt.

questions.question: Explain how British policies affected Indian farmers.
questions.answer: British land revenue policies placed heavy taxes on farmers and created economic difficulties.

questions.question: Explain why land revenue systems caused problems for peasants.
questions.answer: High revenue demands forced peasants to borrow money and fall into debt.

questions.question: Explain the relationship between zamindars and peasants.
questions.answer: Zamindars collected revenue from peasants and paid a fixed amount to the British government.

questions.question: Explain why indigo was grown mainly in Bengal.
questions.answer: Bengal had suitable soil and climate for indigo cultivation.

questions.question: Explain how indigo cultivation affected food production.
questions.answer: Indigo cultivation reduced the land available for growing food crops.

questions.question: Explain the importance of the Indigo Revolt in Indian history.
questions.answer: The Indigo Revolt showed the power of peasant resistance against unfair colonial policies.

questions.question: Explain how British land policies changed rural life in India.
questions.answer: British policies introduced new revenue systems that changed the relationship between landowners and farmers.

questions.question: Explain why studying the Indigo Revolt is important.
questions.answer: Studying the Indigo Revolt helps us understand the struggles of farmers under colonial rule.
